text: Cabin crew should provide passengers with individualized briefings on aircraft safety features as needed and in accordance with local regulations.
Cabin crew are not expected to provide assistance with eating, taking medication, or activities within the lavatory.

section: Mobility Aid Delivery at Transfer Points
text: Checked mobility aids should be delivered as close as possible to the aircraft door within the shortest possible time, depending on airport facilities, connection times, and retrieval requirements. If this service is unavailable, passengers who disclosed their mobility aid during booking should be notified as applicable and feasible.

section: Ground Staff Handling of Mobility Aids
text: When a passenger communicates the use of a mobility aid at the time of booking, ground staff should be prepared to return the aid upon disembarkation and load it after the passenger has boarded the aircraft.
For interline connections, the delivering airline's responsibility for assisting a passenger with disabilities ends once the passenger has been transferred to and accepted by the connecting airline.

section: Technical Communication for Mobility Aid Transport
text: Ground staff and airport assistance teams from both delivering and connecting airlines must communicate timely with ramp teams regarding mobility aids, providing at minimum the weight, dimensions, and battery type.

section: Coordination for Delayed Mobility Aid Return
text: If a mobility aid cannot be returned during a connection, the handling teams of the delivering and connecting airlines should coordinate to share ground equipment and knowledge regarding the best handling and support approach.

If connecting airline or airport assistance staff are not present, delivering airline personnel should make necessary arrangements for the passenger's care and welfare until a seamless handover is ensured.

section: Transition from Aisle Chairs to Airport Chairs
text: When a mobility aid is unavailable at the transfer point, carriers and assistance providers should coordinate to minimize the passenger's time in an aisle chair and move them to an airport chair as soon as possible. Note that some individuals cannot use airport chairs that lack seat belts.

Mobility aids are transported in aircraft cargo compartments, which may be configured for either Unit Load Devices (ULDs) or bulk loading. The procedures for transporting mobility aids differ significantly depending on whether the aircraft uses ULDs or bulk loading.
To ensure timely delivery to the passenger, mobility aids should be the last items loaded into the aircraft and the first items unloaded. The established best practice is 'last on, first off'.

Whenever possible, mobility aids should be secured in Unit Load Devices (ULDs). Using ULDs is a best practice because it reduces the risk of damage by minimizing the number of ramp personnel handling the aid and providing protection during the flight.

A Unit Load Device (ULD) is a removable aircraft part subject to strict civil aviation authority requirements regarding design, testing, production, operations, repair, and maintenance. ULDs consist of either an aircraft container or a combination of an aircraft pallet and a pallet net.

Once a mobility aid is in air travel configuration mode, it can be loaded into an aircraft container. Air carriers should ensure sufficient personnel are available to assist with lifting the aid if necessary. Ramp personnel should place the mobility aid upright in the container, engage the drive system, and ensure brakes are engaged if available.

When loading multiple mobility aids in one container, they must be secured so they do not touch each other or the container walls to prevent damage. Personnel should use straps and internal tie-down points or follow specific airline guidance for proper securing techniques.

Containers holding mobility aids should be loaded in the aircraft closest to the cargo door to facilitate quick unloading at the destination. Containers must be closed, flaps secured, and loaded according to airline policy.

To avoid damage, personnel must not over-secure mobility aids by tightening straps too tightly. If loading difficulties occur, a supervisor should be contacted. If a mobility aid cannot be loaded in a container, the air carrier should follow its policy and notify the passenger to determine alternatives. As a best practice, no other commodities should be loaded in a container with a mobility aid.

Airlines and airport operators should ensure sufficient connection time is allotted for both the passenger and their mobility aid. This includes time for the aid to be reassembled and delivered to the passenger at the connection point to facilitate airport mobility, and subsequently disassembled and re-packaged for departure.

Mobility aids must be configured to the smallest possible size to ensure they fit through cargo doors (if loaded in the cargo compartment) or can be stowed in the cabin. Booking agents should utilize aircraft dimension charts to validate this information at the time of booking.

Information regarding the mobility aid—including weight, dimensions, battery type (specifically if lithium-ion), Watt hours, and whether the battery is easily removable—should be collected in advance and communicated to all airlines involved in the passenger's itinerary.
Airlines may request photos to determine if a battery must be removed. However, if the battery is securely attached and well-protected, removal may not be necessary.
Airlines should ensure that their booking paths allow for the capture of mobility aid information and provide the ability to edit this information before travel.

At connection or transfer points, airlines, airports, and assistance providers must implement clear procedures to ensure that mobility aids are delivered to the customer safely and without delay upon request.

Passengers should be able to reserve the seat type that best accommodates their disability via the booking system or through follow-up with the airline. These assignments must comply with the class of service, government regulations, and carrier safety policies.

Airline websites should provide the following information: definitions and booking processes for assistance services; safety and mobility aid space restrictions; conditions for requiring a safety assistant; medical equipment allowances and restrictions; procedures for claiming mishandled mobility aids; and a helpline telephone number.

For manual wheelchairs, the required information includes the weight, dimensions at the smallest ready-to-load size (length x width x height), and whether the passenger intends to stow the device in the cabin.

When specialized facilities are unavailable, mobility aids should be loaded in the aircraft's cargo compartment in a manner that ensures they are easily accessible for timely return to the passenger. Every effort should be made to allow small mobility aids, such as canes, walkers, and crutches, to be stored in the cabin if they are needed during the flight.

Passengers checking in mobility aids should be offered the option of using an airport wheelchair. If infrastructure permits and the passenger prefers, they should generally be allowed to use their own mobility aid up to the aircraft door. This preference must be communicated to and respected by airport assistance providers.

When a mobility aid is checked for transport in the cargo compartment, airlines must ensure that the mobility aid information and passenger assistance requirements are documented in the Passenger Name Record (PNR) using the appropriate Special Service Request (SSR) codes.

Airlines should make every effort to promptly add any missing information regarding a passenger's mobility aid to ensure proper planning and loading. If information is present, airlines must validate that the SSR code is correct and that the wheelchair details are available to the relevant teams.

Mobility aid and assistance information must be transmitted well in advance to all stakeholders involved in the journey to allow for proper equipment and staffing planning. Specifically, the cabin crew and the captain in command must be informed of the passenger's needs and the location of the mobility aid within the cargo compartment.

Ground staff are responsible for recording the passenger's preference for the delivery and return of their mobility aid upon arrival, specifying whether it should be delivered at the gate or the baggage claim area.

The default procedure is to accompany the passenger in their mobility aid up to the gate and, where feasible, to the aircraft door during boarding. However, passengers must be allowed to request that their aid be returned to them at the baggage claim area upon arrival.

Ground staff must complete a mobility aid tag whenever an aid is stored in the cargo compartment. If electronic tags are used, they should be linked to the PNR to enable real-time tracking of the mobility aid throughout the journey.
Whether a mobility aid is checked at the desk or the gate, ground staff must ensure that any components removed from the aid are documented on the mobility aid tag and stowed in the aircraft cabin.

Airlines, airports, handlers, and assistance providers must establish clear, specific procedures for moving mobility aids between the gate and the ramp for every terminal and gate, including the designation of the person responsible for the movement.

Technical documentation for mobility aids may include: dimensions in air travel and use configurations, illustrations of these configurations, the location of latch mechanisms for component removal, battery details (type, chemistry, and quantity), battery isolation switch locations, drive disengagement mechanisms, allowable tie-down and strapping points, and basic instructions for ramp personnel.

To prevent loss or damage, passengers should be encouraged to remove detachable parts from their mobility aid and carry them into the cabin. Airline operators should provide plastic bags for these components if the passenger does not have any. These bags should be returned directly to the passenger; if that is not possible, they should be handed to the appropriate cabin crew members.

Protecting the joystick is the highest priority during transport. If the joystick is designed for removal, removing the assembly is encouraged. Alternatively, passengers may provide their own methods to protect the joystick.

With passenger approval, airlines should accommodate requests to remove other components that could be lost or damaged, such as seat and back cushions, custom seating, and head, arm, trunk, or leg supports. Any protective covering or wrapping provided by the passenger should be applied whenever feasible.

BEST PRACTICE: In consultation with the passenger, remove the joystick if the mobility aid is designed for removal. If the joystick is not designed for removal or the passenger cannot be consulted, ensure the joystick is adequately protected.

Air carriers should verify with passengers where they wish to receive their mobility aid upon arrival or at a transfer point, in accordance with carrier policy. This location must be marked on the mobility aid tag to ensure correct delivery. BEST PRACTICE: Unless otherwise requested, the mobility aid should be returned to the passenger as close to the aircraft door as possible.
Wherever possible, the mobility aid should be returned to the passenger at the point of transfer, unless the passenger specifically requests otherwise.

When transferring a mobility aid from check-in or the gate to the ramp, personnel should: 
1. Ensure the device is in neutral/freewheel mode before moving it. 
2. Use the nearest elevator whenever feasible. 
BEST PRACTICE: Avoid carrying mobility aids down stairs to reduce the risk of dropping or damaging the equipment.

Mobility aids must be secured in the cargo compartment according to aircraft weight and balance requirements and IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations. They can be secured using two methods: loading into a Unit Load Device (ULD) or via bulk loading.
Verify the mobility aid's weight and maximum dimensions (length, width, and height) in its air travel configuration to ensure it can be accommodated in accordance with carrier policy.

Mobility aids must be moved through the cargo door and positioned within the cargo compartment in an upright position. Tipping or laying mobility aids on their side should be avoided to prevent damage to the equipment or injury to ground staff. To ensure the aid fits through the cargo door, height should be reduced as much as possible, utilizing passenger-provided tools or straps when available.

Batteries must be safe for transport according to IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (DGR 2.3.2.2 and 2.3.2.4) and carrier policy. Lithium-ion batteries that are securely attached and protected by the mobility aid's design do not need to be removed. However, if the aid cannot be transported upright, spillable batteries must be removed and placed in approved containers. Spare batteries must be transported strictly in accordance with IATA DGR and carrier policy.

To prepare for transport, use the power isolation switch or disconnect the quick-disconnect power cable to the battery. The mobility aid must be placed in freewheel mode (disengaging the drive system); failure to disengage the drive system can cause considerable damage to internal components during movement.

A single mobility aid may require up to three different types of tags: 1) A customer-provided tag or Air Travel Information/Configuration Card containing specific aid information; 2) An airline-provided string tag (mobility aid tag) detailing dangerous goods information and proper handling; and 3) A standard airline-generated baggage tag (ABT) for routing purposes.

section: Mobility Aid Tagging and Configuration Details
text: When completing the mobility aid tag, operators should consult the passenger and document the following: travel configuration mode (e.g., lowering the seat or reclining the back support), removal of fragile parts for cabin transport, battery type, tie-down/securing points, and confirmation that the drive system is in neutral. Operators should also refer to the RESNA Air Travel Configuration Card if available.

section: Mobility Aid Battery and Type Codes
text: Guidelines for transporting specific mobility aids: WCBW (Wet cell battery) and WCLB (Lithium-ion battery) require advance notification and may require preparation or assembly/disassembly; weight and dimensions must be specified. WCMP refers to manual power chairs, for which weight and dimensions should be specified by bilateral agreement. It is recommended that the code WCMP be reserved for manual chairs without power assistive devices, although many manual chairs now include such devices to reduce energy expenditure for the user.

The Mobility Aids Action Group (MAAG), in collaboration with the RESNA Assistive Technology for Air Travel Standards Committee and associations representing mobility aid manufacturers and rehabilitation professionals, has developed standards for Air Travel Information Cards. These cards are designed to communicate how mobility aids should be configured for both aircraft storage and use.

Industry standards include specifications for mobility aid features designed to reduce the likelihood of damage during cargo hold storage. This is particularly critical for aircraft with shorter height baggage doors, which can make it more difficult to accommodate certain powered mobility aids.

A Mobility Aid Passport is intended as a static set of information that belongs to the mobility aid and connects the device to its owner. This concept has been adopted by public transportation providers throughout Great Britain via a full British Standard established in 2013.

Under the British Standard (BS), a mobility aid passport is defined as a document attached to a wheelchair containing key instructions and information regarding the provisions for the needs of an individual and their wheelchair during transport.

The MAAG recommends that mobility aid passports be developed collaboratively by mobility aid manufacturers, rehabilitation professionals, and after-market equipment providers. Manufacturers and rehabilitation professionals are responsible for the basic elements, while after-market vendors are responsible for updates to ensure the accuracy of the passport content.

Every mobility aid manufacturer and subsequent point of service should ensure that mobility aids are equipped with key information, specifically: whether the aid is manual or battery-powered, and if manual, whether it is equipped with a wheel lock or brake.
It is recommended that the Mobility Aid Passport be aligned with the RESNA AT-1 Section 4 Annex B Air Travel Configuration Card and the RESNA standard AT-112.

Airlines should consider using a Mobility Aid Tag to facilitate communication between passengers with disabilities and the ground staff responsible for handling and loading mobility aids on the day of travel.

Until electronic tags are developed, airlines should provide manual mobility aid tags for checked mobility aids. Suggested information for these tags includes the passenger's name and contact details. If the tag includes a barcode for the baggage system, airlines should associate the tag reference with the passenger's Passenger Name Record (PNR) to facilitate tracking during loading and unloading.

The Mobility Aid Accessibility Group (MAAG) recommends that the back of the mobility aid tag feature a unique, inclusive, and worldwide recognizable design. For example, using a bright neon color can draw attention and reflect inclusivity.

The MAAG recommends that IATA create and implement an accessible standard electronic mobility aid tag across the industry. This e-tag should include a barcode for scanning into the baggage system, eliminating the need for a separate electronic baggage tag.

Airlines should develop a toolkit for passengers traveling with mobility aids. This toolkit should inform passengers about what to expect, what information they must provide, why that information is requested, and provide instructions to facilitate the transportation of their mobility aid.
The passenger toolkit for mobility aids should be made available via the airline's website and through the websites of disability community organizations.

Airlines should use a toolkit to inform passengers of the information required in advance of travel. At a minimum, the Passenger Name Record (PNR) should capture the dimensions of the mobility aid and the battery type. Passengers should be notified of these requirements and the reasons for the request during the booking process.
The IATA Guidance on the Transport of Mobility Aids (First Edition) was published in February 2023.

The information provided by IATA in this document is for general information purposes only and does not constitute a recommendation, endorsement, or preference. Users should seek competent legal counsel and not rely solely on this document for business or individual decisions, as laws and regulations vary by jurisdiction.
The safe handling of mobility aids, including wheelchairs, has been identified as a key priority for advancing air travel accessibility.

The Action Group brings together a comprehensive range of stakeholders, including accessibility organizations, airlines, ground service providers, airports, state regulators, academia, and mobility aid manufacturers.

The objective of this guidance is to improve the safe transportation of mobility aids to reduce the risk of damage and breakage. This is to be achieved through process improvements, the development of new tools, training, industry-wide standardization requirements, and design improvements for mobility aids.

This guidance reflects the best current practices available at the time of drafting. It is intended to be an iterative document that will be revised and expanded as practice improvements are developed into future standards.

IATA and its airline members are committed to supporting the development of best handling practices to achieve the Mobility Aid Action Group (MAAG) vision of making air travel with mobility aids as seamless as possible for all customers.

This guidance serves as reference material to facilitate the safe transportation of mobility aids. It is not definitive; users must adhere to the applicable laws, regulations, and procedures of their specific jurisdiction or place of operations.

Contributing airlines include Aerolineas Argentina, Air Canada, Air New Zealand, Alaska Airline, British Airways, Delta, flydubai, ITA, and United Airlines. Contributing airports include Aena, Amsterdam Schiphol Airport, and Cincinnati / Northern Kentucky International Airport.

Accessibility: The right of access for persons with disabilities, enabling the use of assistive technology so they can perform necessary tasks in a similar amount of time and effort as individuals without disabilities.

Air Travel Configuration Card: A card provided by the passenger to the airline containing visual images, illustrations, or diagrams of a mobility device configured for air travel. These cards are designed by RESNA and provided by the mobility aid manufacturer where available.
Airline Operator: The ground handler appointed by the airline to manage operations on their behalf.

Ambulift: A medical high loader or high lifter (also called an ambulance lift or ambilift) used as a boarding vehicle for passengers with disabilities and reduced mobility (PRM), primarily those using wheelchairs (WCHC) or elderly passengers with difficulty climbing stairs.

Cargo straps: A basic tie-down unit consisting of flat woven textile webbing (with one fixed end and one adjustable end), a tensioning device, and two end fittings, used for the restraint of cargo on board civil transport aircraft (ref. ISO).

Dangerous Goods: Articles or substances capable of posing a hazard to health, safety, property, or the environment, which are listed in the IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (DGR) or classified according to the DGR.

Dry Batteries: Sealed, non-vented batteries containing zinc salts and other solids, or those of the nickel cadmium type or other metal combinations. Examples include alkali-manganese, zinc-carbon, nickel-metal hydride (NiMh), and nickel-cadmium (NiCad) batteries.

Lithium-ion batteries (Li-ion): A type of secondary (rechargeable) battery where lithium is present in ionic form in the electrolyte; this category includes lithium polymer batteries. They are generally used to power devices such as mobile telephones, laptop computers, and e-bikes.
Load Distribution Message (LDM): A brief message used in IATA Airport Handling Manual procedures to communicate the cargo load distribution in an aircraft to the transit station.
Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America (RESNA): A standards organization that develops standards for assistive technology for mobility aids.
Mobility Aid Passport: A static set of information provided by the mobility aid manufacturer that connects the mobility aid to its owner.

Mobility Aid Tag: A physical tag attached to a mobility aid to provide airlines with the information necessary for safe transport. These tags are generally provided and completed by airlines, but may originate from the passenger or a disability organization.
Freewheel mode refers to placing a mobility aid into a state where the drive mechanism and/or brakes are disengaged, allowing the aid to be manually pushed or moved.

Non-spillable batteries utilize an absorbed electrolyte (such as absorbed glass mat (AGM), gel battery, gel cell, sealed lead-acid (SLS), or dry/dry cell) and do not leak electrolyte or liquid even if the battery case is ruptured or cracked. These batteries must be capable of passing specific vibration and pressure differential tests.

Disability is a term referring to individual functioning, encompassing physical, sensory, cognitive, intellectual, and mental impairments, neurodevelopmental conditions, and various chronic diseases. Non-visible disabilities are defined as those that are not immediately apparent.
Passengers with disabilities include, but are not limited to, people with reduced mobility (PRM), people who are blind or have low vision, and people who are deaf or hard of hearing.

Passengers with disabilities include people with speech disabilities, intellectual and developmental disabilities, and cognitive disabilities (including mental health conditions). It also includes people with illnesses authorized for travel by medical authorities whose mobility is impaired by a pathology in progress, and people unable to stand or walk due to injury.

Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM) are defined as any person whose mobility is reduced due to physical disability (locomotory or sensory), intellectual impairment, age, illness, or any other cause of disability, and who requires special accommodation or assistance beyond that provided to other passengers.

The need for PRM assistance is identified through special requests made by the passenger, their family, a medical authority, or reports from airline personnel and industry-associated persons (e.g., travel agents). The level of assistance required from airports and air carriers varies based on individual needs.
A Personal Care Attendant provides assistance to a passenger that cabin crew are unable to provide.

IATA Resolutions are binding for member airlines. Adopting, changing, or rescinding a resolution requires unanimous support from all participating airlines. Resolutions are developed by an owning group, endorsed by the Board, and adopted by the Conference with unanimous support.
IATA Recommended Practices provide guidance to members. They are developed by an owning group, endorsed by the Board, and adopted by the Conference with a two-third majority support from airlines.

A Safety Assistant must be at least 16 years old (unless local legislation specifies otherwise) and must be physically and mentally capable and willing to assume responsibility for a passenger with a disability (PWD) during an emergency.

Spillable batteries consist of metal plates immersed in a corrosive liquid electrolyte, such as dilute sulphuric acid. These batteries will leak the corrosive electrolyte if they are not maintained in an upright position throughout transport, including during loading and unloading.

Whenever possible, mobility aids other than battery-powered aids should be stored in the aircraft cabin. Manual wheelchairs that cannot be collapsed to fit in the cabin must be stored in the cargo compartment; however, any removable components should be returned to the passenger for cabin storage to minimize the risk of damage.

The presence of passengers with disabilities, their personal care attendants, and safety assistants, as well as any specific on-board arrangements made for them, should always be communicated to the appropriate crew members, such as the pilot-in-command or the senior cabin crew member.
Passengers with disabilities, along with their personal care attendants and safety assistants, should be offered opportunities for pre-boarding.
Airlines should consider requesting photos and other supporting information from passengers to determine whether a mobility aid battery needs to be removed.

If passengers must be offloaded for any reason, the highest possible priority for transportation should be given to passengers with disabilities, as well as their personal care attendants and safety assistants.

Passengers who require a lift and transfer to board the aircraft, including those using aisle chairs, should be allowed to pre-board with sufficient time before general boarding begins to ensure privacy and dignity during the transfer process.
Airlines and assistance providers should maintain specialized mobility teams specifically trained to provide lifts and assist passengers with the transfer from an aisle chair to the aircraft seat.

When an aircraft is on a remote stand or boarding bridges are unavailable, mobility aid users generally must be transferred out of their mobility aid on the ground outside the aircraft or within the vehicle that transported them to the aircraft.

When jet bridges are unavailable, various boarding methods should be employed, such as scissor elevators, ambulifts, stair climbers, stair elevators, or mobile passenger boarding ramps (where permitted by regulation).

Airports should provide a variety of appropriate boarding equipment, including ramps, lifts, and ambulifts. Airlines and airports must coordinate to ensure that assistance teams are alerted and the necessary mobility aid loading equipment is available in time for boarding.

Depending on local regulations, airlines and airports should coordinate the provision of lifting slings, transfer boards, and transfer belts to ensure a safe and dignified lift. Passengers should also be permitted to use their own personal transfer aids, as standard equipment provided by airports and airlines may not be suitable for all individuals.
Upon boarding, passengers with disabilities should be provided with assistance to store their carry-on baggage.
